planting.note: Plant container-grown seedlings or place a partly husked seed nut on its side with one-third buried in moist, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed; coconut palm is strictly limited to frost-free zones and cannot survive freezing temperatures even briefly.

directions: Plant container-grown seedlings in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; coconut palm is the most widely cultivated and economically important palm tree in the world — a single tree provides coconut fruit (with meat, milk, water, and oil), fibrous husk (coir), palm fronds (for thatching and weaving), and palm heart, making it one of the most useful food trees in human history. In zone 10–12 Florida, Hawaii, and tropical coastal areas it grows to 30–60 feet with a gracefully curving trunk and a spreading canopy of long, feathery fronds. Trees begin producing coconuts 6–10 years after planting and can remain productive for 70–80 years. Excellent salt tolerance makes it one of the best trees for coastal landscapes. 'Malayan Dwarf' cultivars are shorter, earlier fruiting, and moderately hurricane-resistant. Growth is faster in deep, moist, sandy soil with access to groundwater. Extremely frost-sensitive — any freezing temperature causes permanent damage; restricted to southernmost Florida and Hawaii in the US.
sources: University of Hawaii at Manoa Cooperative Extension

planting.note: Divide clumps by separating rhizomes with a sharp spade in spring or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; red ginger blooms best in bright light but tolerates the partial shade under high-canopy trees in zone 10–12 landscapes.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; red ginger (Ostrich plume ginger, pink cone ginger) is one of the most visually dramatic tropical ornamentals available, producing tall, cane-like stems bearing long, glossy, banana-like leaves and topped by spectacular upright or arching flower spikes with densely packed, overlapping bracts in vivid scarlet-red, pink, or white — the bracts (not petals) are the showy feature, lasting for months as both living spikes and cut flower material. The plant is the dominant decorative ginger seen in Hawaiian lei-making and floral arrangements worldwide. Small bulbils often develop in the flower head after bloom and can be removed and replanted. In zone 10–12 it is essentially everblooming. Excellent combined with bird of paradise, heliconias, and tropical foliage plants for a lush, tropical garden effect.
sources: University of Hawaii at Manoa Cooperative Extension

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and set out after all frost danger has passed in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; melampodium requires full sun for best performance and may become leggy or fail to bloom well in partial shade.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and set out after frost in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; melampodium (butter daisy, black-foot daisy annual) is a Central American native that is one of the most heat-tolerant, drought-resistant, and reliably self-cleaning annual daisies available for high-summer performance in the South — the cheerful, bright golden-yellow, one-inch daisy flowers are produced in extraordinary abundance from late spring until frost on dense, mounded, intensely bushy plants. Unlike many summer annuals, melampodium needs no deadheading, tolerates intense heat and humidity, performs well in poor, dry soil, and maintains its compact form without pinching. An exceptional performer where impatiens and petunias fail in midsummer. Excellent in mass plantings, borders, and containers. 'Million Gold' (compact, 10–12 inches), 'Showstar' (heat-tolerant, 18 inches), and 'Derby' (uniform, compact) are widely grown commercial selections.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in partial shade after last frost; Bolivian begonia performs best in bright indirect light to partial shade — full sun bleaches and scorches the flowers and foliage.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in partial shade; Bolivian begonia is the wild species parent behind many of the most popular trailing tuberous begonia hybrids (Begonia × tuberhybrida), bearing gracefully pendulous, tubular to saucer-shaped flowers of brilliant vermilion-red to orange in a continuous cascade from late spring until frost — hummingbirds are strongly attracted to the tubular flowers. Unlike wax begonias, Bolivian begonia has a naturally trailing, elegant branching habit that makes it exceptional in hanging baskets, tall containers, and window boxes where stems can arch downward. In zones 9–11 the plant overwinters as a small tuberous perennial. Excellent cultivars derived from this species include the celebrated 'Bonfire' (orange-red, excellent heat tolerance) and the 'Santa Cruz' series. Tubers can be dug before frost in colder zones and stored dry over winter.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide corms in spring when soil temperatures reach 65°F or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; giant elephant ear requires ample moisture and rich soil to produce its spectacular oversized foliage — drought stress immediately reduces leaf size.

directions: Divide corms in spring or plant container specimens in consistently moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; giant elephant ear is the largest-leaved of all the commonly grown elephant ears, producing individual leaves that can reach 5–6 feet long and nearly as wide on robust, upright petioles — each leaf is a single, immense, blue-green to pale-green paddle with a distinctive chalky, glaucous bloom on the upper surface. The effect of a mature clump, with 6–10 massive leaves arching outward like an exploding green fountain, is uniquely dramatic in the garden. Thrives in consistently moist to wet soil and at pond and stream margins. Goes fully dormant in winter but corms are cold-hardy to zone 8 when heavily mulched. The popular 'Thailand Giant' selection is selected for maximum leaf size. Distinct from Alocasia macrorrhiza and Colocasia esculenta — this species has the palest, most glaucous, most impressive foliage of the three. Excellent for tropical-look gardens in the Gulf South.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
